classification.domain: hardware-rooted security for IoT and AI systems

topic.what: Comprehensive survey reviewing PUF-based and hardware-rooted trust mechanisms for IoT device authentication and AI model integrity protection
topic.how: Systematically examines and compares TPM-based attestation, silicon/FPGA PUFs, hybrid container-aware roots of trust, and software-only approaches; evaluates trade-offs in security strength, scalability, cost, and deployment complexity
topic.why_matters: IoT devices face hardware-level threats (cloning, counterfeiting, supply-chain manipulation) in physically exposed environments; traditional cryptographic approaches with non-volatile key storage are vulnerable to extraction and impose prohibitive overhead for resource-constrained edge devices; AI model parameters on edge devices need hardware-anchored protection against tampering and replacement

characteristics.what_it_measures: PUF response repeatability across environmental variations (temperature, voltage, aging)
characteristics.value: parametric (varies by PUF type and conditions)
characteristics.vs_baseline: Traditional key storage provides deterministic response
characteristics.evidence: SRAM PUF sensitivity to temperature fluctuations, voltage variations, and device aging; RO-PUF requires calibration and oscillator-selection strategies
characteristics.context: Survey reviews reliability degradation patterns including neighboring-cell effects and temporal evolution

characteristics.vs_baseline: No differentiation between devices if collisions occur
characteristics.evidence: Goal is minimizing probability of response collisions across chips from same fabrication process
characteristics.context: Core PUF metric enabling device-specific identification and authentication

characteristics.what_it_measures: Resistance to machine learning-based surrogate model construction from observed CRPs
characteristics.vs_baseline: Unprotected strong PUFs vulnerable to SVM, neural network, and evolutionary optimization attacks
characteristics.evidence: ML-resistant PUF designs using nonlinear architectures, erasable CRPs, and deception-based protocols reduce attacker prediction accuracy
characteristics.context: Increasingly critical as AI capabilities improve; paper reviews deception-based authentication and adversarial ML techniques for defense

characteristics.what_it_measures: Correct device identification and forgery detection rates
characteristics.value: 91.7% authentication accuracy, up to 98.75% forgery detection
characteristics.evidence: Deep-learning-enhanced optical PUF for wearable devices; PUF-based authentication protocols reviewed in Table 1
characteristics.context: Varies by PUF type (electronic, optical, nanomaterial-based); more recent optical and AI-resilient designs show improved performance

characteristics.what_it_measures: Suitability for large-scale IoT deployments with resource-constrained devices
characteristics.value: mixed - PUF-based and hybrid approaches show promise; software-only insufficient
characteristics.vs_baseline: Software-only trust mechanisms insufficient in adversarial settings
characteristics.evidence: Survey concludes PUF-based and hybrid trust anchors offer promising balance for large-scale AI-enabled IoT systems
characteristics.context: Considers scalability, cost, deployment complexity trade-offs; hybrid designs extend hardware trust into containerized environments

characteristics.what_it_measures: Capability to bind AI model parameters to legitimate hardware and verify runtime integrity
characteristics.vs_baseline: No hardware binding for AI models
characteristics.evidence: PUF-based trust anchors enable unclonable device authentication, model binding, secure boot, and runtime integrity checks for edge AI
characteristics.context: Identified as critical requirement for secure edge intelligence but with insufficient integration of PUF-based trust with AI workload verification

not_for: environments with extremely tight power budgets (public-key cryptography still needed)
applications requiring deterministic response with zero noise tolerance
highly regulated systems requiring standardized certification
requires: error correction for unstable PUF responses

novelty: Structured synthesis of SRAM-based PUFs integrating fabrication process variations, reliability behavior, and ML-based authentication
Comprehensive comparison framework evaluating trust anchor mechanisms across six criteria: anchor type, key storage model, scalability, resource suitability, AI integrity support, and overhead
Integration of PUF security with AI model integrity verification for edge AI deployment
Review of ML-driven attacks and defenses including deception-based authentication protocols and adversarial PUF poisoning
open_problems: Absence of standardized benchmarking and threat modeling frameworks for PUF security evaluation
Limited robustness against increasingly sophisticated ML-based modeling attacks as AI capabilities advance
Insufficient integration of PUF-based trust anchors with AI workload integrity verification mechanisms
Unresolved supply-chain-aware Trojan detection and lifecycle security assurance challenges
Environmental reliability under long-term aging and extreme conditions
Standardization and deployment feasibility for large-scale IoT
